comments:
Summary:
This paper proposes a joint log-linear model to simultaneously select salient phrases (candidate keyphrases) and predict argumentative discourse relations in goal-oriented spoken meetings. The model integrates content-only, discourse-only and joint features, supports latent discourse relations when annotations are unavailable, and is trained with a SampleRank-based algorithm. Inference alternates between discourse and content optimization (message-passing + ILP). Experiments on AMI and ICSI meeting corpora show improvements over SVM baselines and a neural discourse parser on phrase selection, discourse prediction, ROUGE-based extractive summarization, and a downstream task (consistency-of-understanding prediction).

Strengths:
- The joint modeling idea is well-motivated: content and discourse can inform each other, and the paper presents a concrete instantiation leveraging both sources.
- A practical latent-discourse variant increases applicability when gold discourse labels are scarce.
- Evaluation is comprehensive: phrase selection, discourse labeling, extractive summaries (ROUGE), and an applied prediction task (COU). Results indicate consistent gains over reasonable baselines.
- The paper includes useful feature engineering and some analysis of learned features and error cases.

Weaknesses / Concerns:
- Attachment structure assumption: the model assumes the discourse attachment tree is given during training and testing, or uses a heuristic to construct it when unavailable. This is a strong assumption for real-world deployment; performance sensitivity to attachment errors is not reported.
- Candidate phrase gold labels are induced by head-word overlap with abstractive/participant summaries. This heuristic may be noisy and could bias evaluation; more discussion of its limitations would help.
- The discourse-labeled training set (AMI-SUB) is relatively small (129 discussions). It is unclear how robust the learned discourse predictions are and whether gains come mainly from content features or from joint modeling—more ablations would clarify this.
- Comparison baselines: the paper compares to linear SVMs and one neural parser. Given the 2017 timeframe and growing use of neural approaches, stronger or end-to-end neural baselines for phrase selection and discourse prediction would strengthen claims of state-of-the-art performance.
- Computational cost and scalability (training/inference time, ILP scaling with many candidates) are not reported.

Suggestions for improvement / future work:
- Evaluate robustness to imperfect or automatically produced attachment trees; report performance when using noisy attachments.
- Provide ablation studies isolating the contributions of content, discourse, and joint features more thoroughly, and analysis of latent-discourse vs. gold-discourse performance.
- Consider semantic clustering of phrase variants (to address inconsistent predictions for paraphrases) or incorporate contextual embeddings to reduce reliance on head-word matching.
- Report runtime and scalability behavior, and discuss potential for end-to-end neural architectures that jointly infer attachments as well as relations and salient content.

Conclusion:
Overall this is a solid, well-executed paper addressing an important problem in meeting analysis. The joint modeling approach and the latent-discourse variant are useful contributions, and the empirical evaluation is reasonably thorough. I recommend acceptance (strong), provided the authors address or discuss the attachment-structure assumption and the limitations of the phrase-labeling heuristic.
